subject: History
title: Nazism and the Rise of Hitler

questions.text: The International Military Tribunal was set up at:
questions.explanation: At the end of the war, an International Military Tribunal at Nuremberg was set up to prosecute Nazi war criminals.

questions.text: Which country was the leading member of the Axis Powers?
questions.explanation: The Axis powers consisted of Germany, Italy, and Japan.

questions.text: The Weimar Republic was established in Germany after:
questions.explanation: The defeat of Imperial Germany in WWI and the abdication of the Emperor led to the birth of the Weimar Republic.

questions.text: What was the name of the peace treaty signed by Germany after WWI?
questions.explanation: The peace treaty at Versailles with the Allies was a harsh and humiliating peace for Germany.

questions.text: The 'November Criminals' was a term used for:
questions.explanation: Those who supported the Weimar Republic, mainly Socialists, Catholics and Democrats, were mockingly called 'November Criminals'.

questions.text: A situation where prices rise phenomenally high is called:
questions.explanation: In 1923, Germany faced hyperinflation where the value of the Mark fell and prices of goods soared.

questions.text: The Wall Street Exchange crashed in 1929 in which country?
questions.explanation: The crash of the Wall Street Exchange in the USA marked the start of the Great Depression.

questions.text: Adolf Hitler was born in:
questions.explanation: Hitler was born in 1889 in Austria.

questions.text: The Nazi party was originally known as the:
questions.explanation: Hitler joined a small group called the German Workers’ Party and later renamed it the National Socialist German Workers’ Party.

questions.text: In the 1932 elections, the Nazi Party became the largest party with ______ votes.
questions.explanation: By 1932, it had become the largest party with 37 per cent votes.

questions.text: Who offered the Chancellorship to Hitler on 30 January 1933?
questions.explanation: President Hindenburg offered the Chancellorship, the highest position in the cabinet of ministers, to Hitler.

questions.text: The Enabling Act of 1933 gave Hitler the power to:
questions.explanation: The Enabling Act established dictatorship in Germany, allowing Hitler to ignore parliament and rule by decree.

questions.text: The secret state police of the Nazis was called:
questions.explanation: The Gestapo was the feared secret state police of the Nazi regime.

questions.text: Who was Hitler's economist responsible for economic recovery?
questions.explanation: Hjalmar Schacht was assigned the responsibility of economic recovery through state-funded work-creation programmes.

questions.text: Hitler pulled out of the League of Nations in:
questions.explanation: Hitler pulled Germany out of the League of Nations in 1933.

questions.text: World War II began when Germany invaded:
questions.explanation: In September 1939, Germany invaded Poland, which started the war with France and England.

questions.text: What was the 'Tripartite Pact'?
options: Pact between Germany, Italy, and Japan
Pact between UK, USA, and France
questions.explanation: The Tripartite Pact was signed in 1940 between Germany, Italy and Japan, strengthening Hitler's claim to international power.

questions.text: Hitler’s ideological worldview was based on the ideas of:
questions.explanation: Hitler borrowed and twisted the ideas of Darwin and Spencer regarding 'survival of the fittest' to justify racial hierarchy.

questions.text: In the Nazi racial hierarchy, who were at the top?
questions.explanation: Nordic German Aryans were at the top, while Jews were at the bottom as the 'anti-race'.

questions.text: What does 'Lebensraum' mean?
questions.explanation: Lebensraum or 'living space' was Hitler's geopolitical concept to acquire new territories for resources and settlement.

questions.text: Which of these groups was considered 'undesirable' by Nazis?
questions.explanation: Nazis wanted a society of 'pure and healthy' Aryans; all others were deemed 'undesirable'.

questions.text: At what age were German boys required to join the 'Jungvolk'?
questions.explanation: At ten, boys had to enter Jungvolk; at 14, they joined Hitler Youth.

questions.text: The Nazi ideology regarding women emphasized:
options: Being good mothers and rearing pure-blooded Aryans
questions.explanation: Women were taught to be good mothers, maintain the purity of the race, and distance themselves from Jews.

questions.text: The 'Final Solution' was a Nazi code name for:
questions.explanation: Final Solution was the code for the mass killing of Jews in gas chambers.

questions.text: Who was the propaganda minister of Hitler?
questions.explanation: Joseph Goebbels was the Minister of Propaganda who helped build Hitler's image as a 'saviour'.

questions.text: The gas chambers were mockingly called ______ by the Nazis.
questions.explanation: Gas chambers looked like bathrooms with fake showerheads and were called 'disinfection areas'.

questions.text: The autobiography written by Hitler while in prison is:
questions.explanation: Mein Kampf (German for 'My Struggle') outlines Hitler's political ideology and future plans.

questions.text: What was the result of the Nazi 'Euthanasia Programme'?
options: Killing of many Germans considered mentally or physically unfit
questions.explanation: Under this programme, many Germans who were considered 'unworthy of life' were condemned to death.

questions.text: Why was the German economy hit hardest during the Great Depression?
options: Their recovery depended on short-term loans from the USA which were withdrawn
They spent all money on war
questions.explanation: German industrial recovery was built on US loans; when the Wall Street crash happened, these loans stopped.

questions.text: The Nuremberg Laws of 1935 focused on:
questions.explanation: The laws defined who was a German and who was a Jew, stripping Jews of their rights and citizenship.

questions.text: Article 48 of the Weimar Constitution gave the President the power to:
questions.explanation: Article 48 was an inherent flaw in the Weimar Republic that allowed for the concentration of power.

questions.text: Which of these events helped Hitler gain total power?
questions.explanation: All these events created a vacuum and opportunity for Hitler to dismantle democracy.

questions.text: What happened to Hitler at the end of WWII?
options: He committed suicide in his bunker
questions.explanation: In April 1945, Hitler, his family and Goebbels committed suicide in his Berlin bunker.

questions.text: German children were taught that Jews were:
questions.explanation: Stereotypes about Jews were taught in schools to instill hatred from a young age.

questions.text: The term 'Evacuation' was a Nazi code for:
questions.explanation: Evacuation meant taking people to the 'killing centres' or gas chambers.

questions.text: How did many ordinary Germans react to the Holocaust?
questions.explanation: While many were Nazis, a large population remained passive through fear or apathy, and some braved death to resist.

questions.text: Pastor Niemöller is famous for his writing about:
options: The silence of the masses during Nazi atrocities
questions.explanation: He wrote about how people didn't speak up as the Nazis took away various groups one by one.

questions.text: What does the 'Holocaust' refer to?
options: The state-sponsored mass murder of Jews and others by Nazis
questions.explanation: The Holocaust is the term for the systematic genocide of approximately 6 million Jews.

questions.text: After WWI, Germany had to pay a war indemnity of:
questions.explanation: Germany was forced to pay compensation amounting to £6 billion under the Treaty of Versailles.

questions.text: The 'Reichstag' was the name of the German:
questions.explanation: The Reichstag was the German Parliament, which was burned in a mysterious fire in 1933.
